stories.headline: Iran war escalates sharply: UNIFIL peacekeeper killed, Iran targets officials' homes, Trump floats seizing Kharg Island
stories.body: The Iran conflict entered a dangerous new phase over the weekend as Iran's joint military command declared the private residences of US and Israeli officials to be legitimate targets. A UN peacekeeper from UNIFIL was killed and another critically injured when a projectile struck their position in southern Lebanon late on March 29, with the origin under investigation. Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu ordered the military to 'further expand' its security zone in Lebanon to counter Hezbollah, while an Israeli airstrike in southern Lebanon killed at least 11 people. President Trump claimed Iran had agreed to allow 20 oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz, calling it a 'sign of respect,' and openly mused about 'taking the oil' and seizing Kharg Island — Iran's key export hub — a move analysts warn could dramatically widen the war. The IAEA confirmed 'severe damage' to Iran's heavy water production plant at Khondab, rendering it 'no longer operational.' Iran struck a power station in Kuwait, killing an Indian worker, and Bahrain reported intercepting 174 missiles and 391 drones since Iranian attacks began on February 28. Pakistan continued its mediation push, offering to facilitate direct US-Iran talks.

stories.headline: IRGC and Iranian president clash over war strategy as regime recruits volunteers for ground defence
stories.body: A deepening rift has emerged between Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian and senior Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps commanders over the conduct and cost of the war. Reports indicate IRGC Commander Brigadier General Ahmad Vahidi is dismissing Pezeshkian's concerns about deteriorating economic conditions, while the regime has launched a nationalist recruitment campaign called 'Janfada' (Sacrificing Life) to enlist volunteers in case of a US ground invasion. Iranian officials claimed 281 students and teachers have been killed in US-Israeli strikes since the war began. The parliament speaker accused the US of pursuing a covert 15-point plan after 'failing militarily' and warned of a 'decisive' response to any ground attack. Ukrainian President Zelensky alleged Russia is providing Iran with satellite imagery of US military assets to assist in targeting.

stories.headline: Oil surges past $115 as war drives worst monthly stock crash in Japan since 2008
stories.body: Global markets convulsed as the Iran war's economic shock waves intensified. Brent crude futures jumped 2.8 percent to $115.73 a barrel and US West Texas Intermediate rose 3.1 percent to $102.77, driven by Houthi attacks on Israel and the continuing effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz — now the largest crude supply shock in history, with production losses running at 11 million barrels per day. Japan's Nikkei share average fell about 5 percent, its biggest monthly drop since the 2008 global financial crisis, as stagflation fears spread. US consumer sentiment dropped to a three-month low on inflation worries. Goldman Sachs now expects Brent to average $110 through March and April, easing to $71 by Q4 if disruptions don't worsen — but warned prices could exceed the 2008 record high in an extreme scenario. The IMF projects global growth at 3.3 percent for 2026 but flagged escalating geopolitical tensions as the primary downside risk.

stories.headline: Italy rejects judiciary reform in referendum setback for Meloni government
stories.body: Italian voters rejected a constitutional reform of the judiciary in a referendum held on March 22–23, dealing a significant blow to Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni's government. The reform would have restructured judicial independence and oversight, a long-standing priority for Italy's political right. The result underscores the limits of the governing coalition's ability to push through institutional changes, even with a parliamentary majority. The referendum saw relatively strong turnout compared to recent Italian referendums, suggesting the issue mobilised both supporters and opponents.

stories.headline: CPAC reveals generational split among conservatives over Trump's Iran war
stories.body: The Conservative Political Action Conference exposed a sharp generational divide within the Republican base over the war in Iran. Younger attendees expressed disappointment that Trump had launched military strikes contradicting his long-standing anti-foreign-entanglement rhetoric, while older conservatives defended the campaign as a pragmatic necessity in response to Iranian threats. The split comes as the Pentagon assembles a substantial troop presence in the Middle East, with Republican Senator James Lankford saying he would not dismiss supporting ground troops but emphasised the need for 'clearly defined objectives.' Trump himself has claimed the war has achieved 'regime change' in Iran, a characterisation not supported by events on the ground.

stories.headline: AI deepfakes proliferate in 2026 midterm campaigns as GOP releases fabricated candidate video
stories.body: The National Republican Senatorial Committee released an AI-generated deepfake advertisement featuring a realistic but entirely fabricated version of Democratic Texas Senate candidate James Talarico speaking into the camera for over a minute. The fake Talarico appears to read from old tweets about transgender issues, race, and religion, and makes self-praising comments he never actually said. The ad includes a small 'AI GENERATED' label but experts warn the disclosure is easily missed. It marks the first time a phony version of a candidate has spoken in a lifelike manner for such an extended period in a US campaign. Senator Mark Warner has pushed tech companies to crack down on election deepfakes, and bipartisan calls for federal regulation are growing as researchers warn the technology poses serious risks to voter trust and election integrity.

stories.headline: DHS shutdown becomes longest in history as TSA agents still face pay disruptions
stories.body: The Department of Homeland Security shutdown is now the longest in US history after the House and Senate failed to reconcile competing funding bills over the weekend. While Trump issued an executive order mandating immediate back-pay for TSA agents, the operational disruptions continue: long airport queues persist and ICE identification checks at security checkpoints in several major airports have compounded delays. Democrats are demanding reforms including body-worn cameras for ICE agents and restrictions on enforcement in hospitals, schools, and churches as conditions for any deal. The standoff has no clear resolution in sight as Congress heads into the Semana Santa recess period.

stories.headline: Groundforce indefinite strike begins today at 12 airports as Semana Santa travel starts
stories.body: Ground handling workers at Groundforce began their indefinite strike this Monday at 12 major Spanish airports including Madrid-Barajas, Barcelona-El Prat, Alicante, Valencia, Málaga, Palma de Mallorca, and Bilbao. The first day of stoppages coincides with the start of Semana Santa holiday travel, with partial work stoppages in three daily windows on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. Passengers should expect delays in check-in, boarding, and baggage handling. The disruption compounds further with separate Menzies ground handling walkouts at El Prat confirmed for April 2 to 6. Unions CCOO, UGT, and USO called the action after failed wage negotiations, and the strike could continue through December 31 if no deal is reached.

stories.headline: Spain closes airspace to Iran war-related flights as Sánchez deepens anti-war stance
stories.body: Spain has closed its airspace to flights associated with the war in Iran, a further step in Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez's increasingly prominent anti-war positioning. The closure follows his refusal to permit US use of Spanish military bases for strikes on Iran and his public declaration that the war violates international law. Spain remains the most outspoken European critic of the US-Israel military campaign. The move is likely to draw further friction with Washington but aligns with growing domestic opposition to the conflict, particularly as its economic effects — rising energy prices, inflation at 3.3 percent — are felt directly by Spanish households.

stories.headline: Ibex 35 enters Q2 under pressure as Middle East war drags Spanish markets below 17,000
stories.body: Spain's benchmark Ibex 35 index enters the second quarter struggling to hold above 17,000 points, weighed down by the economic fallout from the Iran conflict. Energy costs continue to climb, with electricity prices for regulated-tariff consumers rising again on Monday. The Spanish paper, cardboard, and cellulose industry has called for emergency state aid to compete with Asian producers, citing the double blow of US tariffs and the energy crisis caused by the Strait of Hormuz closure. Economists warn that the combination of rising input costs and weakening demand could tip Spain into a stagflationary period if the conflict extends into the summer.

stories.headline: Spain's government and Catholic Church to sign protocol compensating abuse victims
stories.body: The Spanish government and the Catholic Church are set to sign a protocol establishing a framework for compensating victims of sexual abuse. The agreement comes after years of pressure from survivor groups and a landmark parliamentary investigation that documented widespread abuse within Church institutions. Separately, the progressive publication Counterpunch highlighted Spain's broader Church-state entanglement, criticising continued public funding under a 1979 agreement with the Holy See signed during the democratic transition. The protocol signing coincides with Palm Sunday and the start of Semana Santa, when the Church's public presence across Spain is at its most visible.

stories.headline: Municipal turf war between Cerdanyola and Sant Cugat blocks 150 youth apartments in Bellaterra
stories.body: A territorial dispute between the municipalities of Cerdanyola del Vallès and Sant Cugat del Vallès is stalling the construction of 150 apartments earmarked for young people in the affluent Bellaterra urbanisation. Bellaterra, home to some 3,000 residents near the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, is recognised as a decentralised municipal entity belonging to Cerdanyola but sits geographically between the two towns. The bureaucratic impasse highlights the chronic shortage of affordable housing for young Catalans, a politically explosive issue as Barcelona-area rents continue to climb despite regulatory interventions. No resolution is in sight as both municipalities claim jurisdiction over the development site.

stories.headline: Poblenou shantytown residents face fresh evictions as encampments keep reappearing
stories.body: Residents of precarious shelters in Barcelona's Poblenou neighbourhood are bracing for new evictions, denouncing that their shantytowns reappear within days of being cleared by police. The cycle of demolition and reconstruction underscores the depth of Barcelona's homelessness crisis and the failure of existing policy responses. Advocates say many residents are migrants with no access to social housing and limited legal protections, while the city argues the encampments pose health and safety risks. The situation has become a recurring flashpoint in local politics, with no party offering a clear solution.

stories.headline: FC Barcelona's new ice rink nears completion, set to open for 2026–27 season
stories.body: FC Barcelona's new ice rink facility, located on University of Barcelona land, is entering its final construction phase and is expected to open at the start of the 2026–27 season. The project has faced repeated delays but club president Joan Laporta is reportedly preparing the opening as a goodwill gesture for Barcelonins. The rink forms part of the broader Espai Barça redevelopment and will serve both the club's ice sports sections and the general public.

stories.headline: Semana Santa begins with Palm Sunday processions and school patios open for community use
stories.body: Barcelona's Semana Santa holiday period kicked off on Palm Sunday with processions across the city and region. Sixteen public school patios across Barcelona have been opened for community use during the holiday week, providing play space for families in dense neighbourhoods with limited parks. The city expects increased visitor numbers over the break, though travel may be complicated by the Groundforce airport strikes. Barcelona's doubled tourist tax takes effect on April 1, just as the holiday crowds arrive.
